questions.question: Section 2(1) of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 defines assault. Which of the following correctly states the two alternative ways of committing assault under s.2(1)?

options.A: Applying force intentionally or recklessly to the body of another person; or threatening to apply such force
options.B: Intentionally or recklessly applying force to or causing impact upon the body of another person; OR threatening by act or gesture (where a person believes force will be applied immediately) to apply force to the body of another person without that person's consent
options.C: Intentionally striking another person; or pointing a weapon at them
options.D: Causing apprehension of immediate force; or actually inflicting force, with malice aforethought

questions.explanation: Section 2(1) of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 creates two routes to liability for assault: (1) Physical assault — where a person intentionally or recklessly applies force to or causes impact upon the body of another person without their consent (or where the person knows or ought to know the other does not consent); (2) Threatened assault — where a person intentionally or recklessly, without lawful excuse, threatens by any act or gesture to apply force to another, in circumstances where the other believes on reasonable grounds that the person is capable of carrying out the threat and will do so immediately. The 1997 Act modernised Irish assault law, abolishing the old common law offences of assault (apprehension-based) and battery (contact-based) and replacing them with a single unified offence under s.2. Options A, C, and D do not accurately capture the statutory wording.

questions.question: Before the NFOATP 1997, Irish law distinguished between 'assault' (causing apprehension of force) and 'battery' (actual application of force). What effect did the 1997 Act have on this distinction?

options.A: The 1997 Act preserved the distinction — assault (apprehension) and battery (contact) remain separate offences
options.B: The 1997 Act abolished the common law distinction between assault and battery; both are now subsumed within the single offence of 'assault' under s.2, which covers both threatened and actual application of force
options.C: The 1997 Act abolished assault but preserved battery as a common law offence
options.D: The 1997 Act created a new offence of 'assault and battery' as a compound offence

questions.explanation: One of the most significant changes effected by the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 was the abolition of the common law distinction between assault (causing another to apprehend immediate application of force) and battery (actual unlawful application of force). The 1997 Act replaced both with the unified offence of 'assault' under s.2, which covers: (a) actual application of force (the old 'battery'), and (b) threats where the victim believes force will immediately be applied (the old 'assault'). This reform brought Irish law into line with the modern approach and eliminated the conceptual confusion created by maintaining two offences for closely related conduct. Option A incorrectly states the pre-1997 distinction survived. Options C and D misstate the 1997 Act's reforms.

questions.question: Under s.2 NFOATP 1997, consent is relevant to assault. Which of the following best describes the role of consent in an assault charge?

options.A: Consent is never relevant to assault — the offence is committed regardless of the victim's consent
options.B: Consent is a complete defence to assault under s.2 — where the complainant consented to the application of force, there is no assault; consent must be genuine, voluntary, and informed
options.C: Consent is only relevant to more serious assault offences (s.3 and s.4), not to the basic assault under s.2
options.D: Consent is relevant but only where the force applied was slight

questions.explanation: Section 2(1) NFOATP 1997 expressly makes consent relevant: the section applies to the application of force to another person 'without the consent of the other person.' Consent is therefore an integral element of the offence — if the complainant genuinely consented to the physical contact, the actus reus of assault is not established. However, consent must be: genuine (not obtained by fraud or misrepresentation), voluntary (not obtained by duress or threats), and informed (the person must understand what they are consenting to). Section 22 NFOATP 1997 provides further rules on consent, including that consent may be implied for everyday social contact (a handshake, a bump in a crowd). The limits on consent (particularly in the context of harm) are addressed in s.3 and s.4 offences. Option A is wrong — consent is expressly relevant under s.2. Options C and D misstate the scope of consent's relevance.

questions.question: Sarah waves her fist in John's face and shouts 'I'm going to hit you right now.' John believes her and is frightened. Sarah does not actually strike John. Has an assault under s.2 NFOATP 1997 been committed?

options.A: No — no physical contact occurred; assault requires actual touching
options.B: Yes — this is threatened assault under s.2(1)(b): Sarah threatened by act or gesture to apply force to John, in circumstances where John reasonably believed she was capable of carrying out the threat immediately
options.C: No — words alone cannot constitute an assault under the 1997 Act
options.D: Yes — but only because the threat was verbal; gestures alone cannot ground assault

questions.explanation: Sarah has committed threatened assault under s.2(1)(b) of the NFOATP 1997. The elements are: (1) Sarah threatened by act or gesture (waving her fist — a gesture) to apply force to John's body; (2) John believed, on reasonable grounds, that Sarah was capable of carrying out the threat immediately; (3) John's reasonable belief that the threat would be carried out immediately. The combination of the gesture (fist-waving) and the verbal threat ('right now') satisfies the requirement that the threat be of immediate force. Under s.2 NFOATP 1997, the threat can be communicated by act, gesture, or words — a significant departure from the old common law debate about whether words alone could constitute an assault. Option A is wrong — threatened assault does not require physical contact. Option C is wrong — the 1997 Act expressly includes threats by act or gesture (which may be supplemented by words). Option D inverts the correct analysis.

questions.question: What is the mental element (mens rea) required for assault under s.2 NFOATP 1997?

options.A: Intention only — recklessness is insufficient for s.2 assault
options.B: Intention or recklessness — s.2(1) expressly states the force must be applied or the threat made 'intentionally or recklessly'
options.C: Negligence — the accused must have been careless
options.D: Strict liability — no mental element is required

questions.explanation: Section 2(1) of the NFOATP 1997 expressly provides that the mens rea for assault is 'intentionally or recklessly.' This means either: (1) Intentional assault — the accused's conscious purpose was to apply force to or threaten the complainant; or (2) Reckless assault — the accused was aware of the risk that their conduct would apply force or create an apprehension of force in the complainant and took that risk unjustifiably. The recklessness standard is subjective (Cunningham recklessness) — the accused must have actually foreseen the risk of the application of force or the threat being perceived. This is an important feature of the 1997 Act's modernisation of assault law. Option A incorrectly excludes recklessness. Option C (negligence) sets a lower threshold not found in s.2. Option D (strict liability) is wrong — s.2 expressly requires intention or recklessness.

questions.question: Section 22 of the NFOATP 1997 addresses consent to everyday physical contact. What principle does it establish?

options.A: Consent is always required for any form of physical contact, however minor
options.B: A person is deemed to consent to ordinary and usual conduct — everyday social contact such as accidentally touching someone in a crowd, a handshake, or a pat on the back does not constitute assault because consent is implied by social norms
options.C: Consent under s.22 must always be in writing to be valid
options.D: Section 22 provides that consent to physical contact must be informed consent as defined in medical law

questions.explanation: Section 22 of the NFOATP 1997 establishes that consent to ordinary and usual conduct is implied in everyday life. Routine social and physical contacts — accidental or intentional touches in crowds, handshakes, pats on the back, sporting contact within normal rules — do not constitute assault because the participants impliedly consent to this level of everyday interaction. This prevents the criminal law from being weaponised against trivial or unavoidable physical contact. The implied consent principle recognises that in organised society, a degree of physical contact is inevitable and consensual without express agreement. Section 22 thus provides the legal mechanism by which ordinary life can proceed without every incidental touch constituting a criminal offence. Option A is wrong — absolute consent is not required for every contact. Option C invents a writing requirement. Option D misapplies informed consent from medical law to everyday contacts.

questions.question: The maximum penalty for basic assault under s.2 NFOATP 1997 on summary conviction is which of the following?

options.B: Class A fine (up to €5,000) and/or 6 months imprisonment on summary conviction in the District Court
options.C: 2 years imprisonment and/or a fine at the court's discretion
options.D: Community service only — assault under s.2 is not imprisonable

questions.explanation: Section 2 assault (the basic assault offence under the NFOATP 1997) is a summary offence triable only in the District Court. On conviction, the maximum penalty is a Class A fine (currently up to €5,000 under the Fines Act 2010) and/or 6 months imprisonment. This reflects the relatively minor nature of the s.2 offence compared to the more serious offences under s.3 (assault causing harm — 5 years; doubled to 10 years by CJ(MP)A 2023) and s.4 (causing serious harm — life imprisonment). The summary-only nature and modest maximum penalty distinguish s.2 assault from the more serious assault offences on the spectrum of non-fatal violence. Options A, C, and D each state incorrect penalty levels.

questions.question: A defendant punches another person in a boxing ring during a licensed amateur boxing match, following the rules of the sport. The recipient of the punch suffers a broken nose. Can the defendant be convicted of assault under s.2 NFOATP 1997?

options.A: Yes — bodily harm was caused and consent to injury is irrelevant
options.B: No — participants in licensed sporting activities impliedly consent to physical contact within the rules of the sport; the punch within the rules of boxing is covered by implied consent and does not constitute unlawful assault
options.C: Yes — a broken nose constitutes assault causing harm under s.3, which cannot be consented to
options.D: No — but only because boxing is a licensed activity; the same punch in an unlicensed fight would be assault

questions.explanation: Participants in licensed sporting activities voluntarily accept the physical contact inherent in their sport, provided it occurs within the rules of that sport. In boxing, punching within the rules is precisely what participants consent to — it is the object of the sport. The consent is genuine, voluntary, and informed (both parties understand they are participating in a contact sport). The implied consent extends to the reasonably foreseeable consequences of participating, including bruising, broken noses, and other injuries that can result from lawful blows. However, consent does not extend beyond the rules — a blow after the bell, a head-butt, or deliberately causing injury outside the rules would not be covered by the sporting consent. The 1997 Act (s.22) and the principle of sporting consent support Option B. Option A is too absolutist. Option C goes beyond the scope of the question (s.3 offence and consent limits). Option D is partially correct in principle but it is the 'within the rules' element, not merely the licensing, that matters.

questions.question: A person threatens to apply force to another person via a text message stating: 'I will hurt you when I see you tomorrow.' Is this a threatened assault under s.2 NFOATP 1997?

options.A: Yes — any communication of a threat to apply force constitutes assault under s.2
options.B: No — the threat is not of immediate force; the required element that the person believes force will be applied 'immediately' is absent where the threat is of future force at a later time; the conduct might constitute a threat to kill (s.5 NFOATP 1997) or harassment, but not s.2 assault
options.C: Yes — modern communications technology means any threat, however remote, now satisfies the immediacy requirement
options.D: No — text messages cannot constitute the actus reus of assault because they are not acts or gestures in person

questions.explanation: The threatened assault under s.2(1)(b) NFOATP 1997 requires that the complainant believes the force will be applied immediately. A threat communicated by text message promising harm 'when I see you tomorrow' lacks this immediacy — the complainant knows the force will not be applied now, but at some future point. The immediacy element is not satisfied. However, such a threat is not without criminal consequence: it may constitute a threat to kill or cause serious injury under s.5 NFOATP 1997 (which does not require immediacy — it requires proof of intent to make the person fear the threat will be carried out), or may constitute harassment under s.10 NFOATP 1997 (as amended by CJ(MP)A 2023). Option A incorrectly ignores the immediacy requirement. Option C disregards the legal significance of immediacy in favour of a technological argument. Option D incorrectly limits the means by which a threat must be communicated.
